coredata.dc:title: Novel up-conversion carbon quantum dots/α-FeOOH nanohybrids eliminate tetracycline and its related drug resistance in visible-light responsive Fenton system

coredata.dc:description: In this study, novel up-converted carbon quantum dots (CQD) incorporated goethite (α-FeOOH) nanohybrids (CQDs/α-FeOOH) were fabricated via a facile hydrolysis method. CQDs/α-FeOOH exhibited remarkably degradation efficiency towards tetracycline (TC, 0.1503 min-1), approximately 3.7 times than pristine α-FeOOH with much lower amount of H2O2 (0.5 mM) under visible light irradiation (λ＞420 nm). The quenching and electron spin resonance (ESR) tests proved ·OH, O2 ·- and 1O2 were involved in TC degradation in CQDs/α-FeOOH catalyzed Fenton system. The potential Ultraviolet-Visible-Infrared (UV–vis-IR) broad-spectrum responsive properties of CQDs/α-FeOOH were proved in degradation various surface charged organic contaminants regarding the function of coupling up-conversion CQDs. CQDs/α-FeOOH also exhibited wide application pH, excellent durability, fairly good catalytic performance within different practical water matrices and completely eliminated related drug resistance of TC even under real solar-light conditions. The good biocompatibility makes CQDs/α-FeOOH served as a promising photocatalyst for remediation of water contamination with efficient utilization of solar energy.

head.ce:title: Novel up-conversion carbon quantum dots/α-FeOOH nanohybrids eliminate tetracycline and its related drug resistance in visible-light responsive Fenton system

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic of possible up-converted mechanism of CQDs enhanced TC degradation in CQDs/α-FeOOH+H2O2 systems under solar spectrum.

In this study, novel up-converted carbon quantum dots (CQD) incorporated goethite (α-FeOOH) nanohybrids (CQDs/α-FeOOH) were fabricated via a facile hydrolysis method. CQDs/α-FeOOH exhibited remarkably degradation efficiency towards tetracycline (TC, 0.1503 min-1), approximately 3.7 times than pristine α-FeOOH with much lower amount of H2O2 (0.5 mM) under visible light irradiation (λ＞420 nm). The quenching and electron spin resonance (ESR) tests proved ·OH, O2 ·- and 1O2 were involved in TC degradation in CQDs/α-FeOOH catalyzed Fenton system. The potential Ultraviolet-Visible-Infrared (UV–vis-IR) broad-spectrum responsive properties of CQDs/α-FeOOH were proved in degradation various surface charged organic contaminants regarding the function of coupling up-conversion CQDs. CQDs/α-FeOOH also exhibited wide application pH, excellent durability, fairly good catalytic performance within different practical water matrices and completely eliminated related drug resistance of TC even under real solar-light conditions. The good biocompatibility makes CQDs/α-FeOOH served as a promising photocatalyst for remediation of water contamination with efficient utilization of solar energy.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the occurrence of water decomposition by TiO2 electrode under visible light in 1972 [1], solar energy as a fascinating non-polluting and abundant energy arises considerably interests regarding the contemporary environmental pollution and energy consumption. In recent years, many strategies like combination with mesoporous materials [2,3], semiconductor modification [4,5], doping [6] are applied to enhance removal efficiency under solar light. Iron (hydr)oxides with wide absorption range from 200 nm to 800 nm tend to photodegrade organic contaminant in aqueous environment, such as azo dyes [7] etc. And α-FeOOH as a prevalent semiconductor, possessing narrow band gap of 2.06 eV, efficient visible light response and stability in heterogeneous photo-Fenton oxidation process, attracts continuous attention due to its abundance, low cost, low toxicity and excellent cooperation with Fenton system [8,9], and widely applied to treat phenolic contaminants [3,8,10], p-chloronitrobenzene [11], dyes [12]. Liu et al. [12] fabricated efficient α-FeOOH loaded graphene oxide-carbon nanotubes aerogel with 99 % Orange II discoloration within 60 min under UV irradiation, However, the catalyst fails to utilize solar energy. Liu et al. [10] reported the degradation of 4-chlorophenol via visible-light driven Fenton system by FeOOH-reduced graphene oxide, it attains 80 % removal efficiency with 6 h and still keep active even after 10 cycles, except that large amounts use of 20 mM H2O2 (molar ratio of H2O2 with 4-chlorophenol of 257). Classical heterogeneous Fenton-like system is generally excited by UV light (∼ 4 % of sunlight spectrum with wavelength of 200−400 nm) [13]. Additionally, poor separation efficiency of photo-generated carriers tend to impair the photocatalytic performance of α-FeOOH [14], and pristine α-FeOOH is still not satisfactory visible light responsive catalyst (43 % of sunlight spectrum with wavelength of 400−800 nm) which can only be trigged by light with the wavelength below 560 nm therefore restricting the utilization of wide spectrum of solar energy. Thus, to overcome those shortcomings, it is definitely significant to modify α-FeOOH or couple it with other optimum materials to obtain efficient solar energy harvest and improve electron transfer to boost its visible responsive property.

Nowadays, up-converted quantum dots materials gradually spring up for their potential application in various fields, like the application of graphitic C3N4 quantum dots grafted rear earth composites in sensors [15], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) based materials apply in photocatalysis field [16], and g-C3N4 quantum dots or its based TiO2 nanohybrids apply in H2 production [17,18], etc. Owing to the excellent cooperation with semiconductor materials, up-converted and efficient, CQDs emerge as desirable alternatives and favorable photocatalytic dopants. CQDs can be excited by the low-energy long wavelengths light (visible and near-infrared light with wavelength of 500−900 nm) and emit high-energy short wavelengths light (UV and visible light with wavelength of 400−550 nm) [19–22], thereby broadening light absorption and responsive ranges. Conjugated π system of CQDs also help to efficiently enhance the carrier separation rates and suppress the recombination of electron-hole pairs [22,23]. Nanosized CQDs ranging from 2 to 10 nm [24], have also drawn tremendous attention by virtue of the ease of preparation, non-toxicity, highly biocompatibility and outstanding electron transport performance [25–27]. These attractive properties of CQDs provide effective approach to enhance sunlight response and photocatalytic performance of metal oxide nanoparticles through composition.

Up to now, several CQDs-based visible light responsive photocatalyst such as TiO2 [28,29], Bi2WO6 [30], BiOX (X = Cl, Br) [31,32], Bi2MoO6 [33], g-C3N4 [34,35], Ag3PO4 [36], Fe3O4 [21], Fe2O3 [9,37,38] have been reported to obviously enhance the photocatalytic efficiencies under visible-light irradiation. CQDs/Ag3PO4 can achieve 2.2 times mineralization of methyl orange dye than pure Ag3PO4 under visible light [36], and exhibit good degradation even under near-infrared light (λ＞700 nm), regrettably, it is not economic enough considering the high cost and high toxicity of Ag element. CQDs/Fe3O4 also shows up to 95 % photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue and outstanding recyclable property under visible light [21], however, up-converted effect of CQDs have not be fully used without the further exploration of utilization of near-infrared light. On the other hand, there were still no reports to explore the CQDs-coupled α-FeOOH nanohybrids and its performance in photo-Fenton oxidation process. Therefore, it is significantly expected to deliver α-FeOOH-based Fenton hybrid catalyst combined with the merits of CQDs to further boost utilization of solar spectrum and form good electron acceptors and donators so as actually overcome the drawbacks of pristine α-FeOOH catalyst. Moreover, the aspect of environmental-friendly and safety of nanoparticles has been highlighted in recent years. Kim’s report showed photocatalysts like TiO2 exhibit cytotoxicity to human cells in different degree [39]. Thus, the potential unfavorable influence or biocompatibility of nanomaterials of CQDs modified α-FeOOH on ecosystem should also be evaluated before largely environmental application.

Here we propose a facile hydrolysis method to obtain visible light-responsive hollow sea urchin-like CQDs modified α-FeOOH nanohybrids (CQDs/α-FeOOH) which proved to possess evident superiority for contaminants removal and energy utilization comparing with the bare α-FeOOH through series of systematic photocatalytic experiments and comprehensive characterization. Tetracycline (TC) was selected to be a target organic contaminant owing to the ubiquitous environmental existence and potential environmental drug resistance issues brought by excessive antibiotic abuse [40]. Moreover, the reactive oxygen species (ROS) involved, possible up-converted mechanism of CQDs, durability, cytotoxicity of as-prepared catalyst and drug resistance of degradation intermediates of TC as well as environmental application in different water matrices under real sunlight irradiation were also explored.

Tetracycline hydrochloride (TC, physiochemical properties shown in Table 1 ) was purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Azo dye Orange II (OII), methylene blue (MB), citric acid, ethylenediamine, ferrous sulphate (FeSO4·7H2O), H2O2 (30 %, v/v), methanol (MeOH) and ascorbic acid (AA) were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Ltd. (Shanghai, China). And other reagents were all analytical grade and used without further purification.

The CQDs were fabricated via a modified hydrothermal method [30]. Briefly, citric acid (5 mmol) was dispersed into 10 mL of deionized water, then 0.335 mL of ethylenediamine was added and stirred 60 min. The above mixture was autoclaved in a Teflon-lined stainless container at 185 ℃ for 5 h under autogenous pressure. Until the sample cooled down to room temperature, the brown solution of CQDs was dialyzed 36 h and then freeze dried at 80 ℃ for 24 h and obtained the CQDs powder. The CQDs/α-FeOOH was synthesized through a hydrolysis process. Certain amount of CQDs (CQDs/α-FeOOH ratio=0 wt. %, 0.7 wt. %, 1 wt. %, 3 wt. % and 5 wt. %) were firstly dissolved into 800 mL of distilled water and keep sonicating for about 30 min to acquire uniform CQDs dispersion. Subsequently, 14.456 g FeSO4·7H2O was added to the above mixture followed by ultrasonication for another 30 min. Finally, The mixture was conducted to hydrolyze at 95 ℃ for 1 h stirring with a magnetic stirrer. As-prepared CQDs/α-FeOOH was filtered and freeze-dried for further use.

The crystal structure of as-prepared CQDs/α-FeOOH samples was characterized through an Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D/Max-2500, Rigaku, Japan), using the Cu Kα source at λ =1.5406 Å and scan ranging from 5° to 70° at a speed of 10° min−1. The surface morphology was observed via the transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100 F, Jeol, Japan). The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra were recorded with a spectrophotometer (UV–vis DRS, U-4100, Hitachi, Japan), BaSO4 as the reference. The zeta potential of CQDs/α-FeOOH was acquired using 0.01 M KNO3 solutions in triplicate, with a Malvern Zetasizer (Malvern instruments limited, USA) as pH function. The functional groups of samples were identified by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 8700 system, PerkinElmer Inc., USA) with the KBr pellet. Raman spectra were obtained with a DXR2 Raman smart (Thermo Scientific) with a 633 nm laser as excitation source. The valence band (VB) of CQDs/α-FeOOH was acquired by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with an Axis UltraDLD spectrometer (Kratos Analytical-A Shimadzu group company, Japan) using a monochromatic Al Kα source (1486.6 eV).

The photocatalytic activity tests were conducted in an open glass reactor with 15 cm caliber and 8 cm height. A 350 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm cutoff filter performed as the visible light source (Vis), and the lamp set 10 cm above the reactor. 50 mg CQDs/α-FeOOH (0.25 g L-1) and H2O2 (0.50 mM) were added into 20 mg L-1 TC solution at natural pH then stirred at 25 ± 1 ℃. The pH of TC solution was adjusted with 0.1 M HCl or NaOH solution. After predetermined intervals, 4 mL suspensions were taken out and centrifuged to remove the photocatalyst. Concentration of H2O2 was calculated as [H2O2] = (A-Ab)/37.4xl through the titanium (IV) oxalate method [41], where A and Ab are the absorbances of the samples and blank solution, respectively, and x is the volume of sample and l is the path length of the spectrophotometer cell in centimeters. For those experiments to study the effect of different organic compounds at different irradiation sources, the initial concentration of MB, OII were all 20 mg L-1. 125 W mercury lamp with 365 nm wavelength (UV, Phillips, China) and 250 W infrared radiation light with wavelength of 760–1100 nm, with main wavelength of 1100 nm (IR, Phillips, China) was used to compared the effect of light sources on TC degradation in this system. The concentrations of MB, OII were obtained at 664, 484 nm by employing the UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-1102, Tianmei Techcomp Ltd, Shanghai, China). Other reaction conditions were same.

The concentration of TC was detected with wavelength set at 357 nm by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, SPD-20 A, Shimadzu, Japan) equipped with C18 column (5 μm, 4.6 × 150 mm) at 27 ℃. Formic acid aqueous solution (0.1 %) and acetonitrile (80:20, v/v) were utilized as the mobile phase with a flow rate of 0.4 mL min-1, injection volume was 30 μL. The mineralization of tetracycline (TC) was analyzed by a total organic carbon (TOC) analyzer (TOC-5050, Shimazdu, Japan). Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were obtained on a Bruker JES FA200 to detect the reactive oxygen species (ROS). The 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) and 2, 2, 6, 6-tetramethylpiperidinooxy (TEMPO) were used as spin-trap reagents. For the indirect analysis of the ROS, radical scavenging experiments were conducted by adding a corresponding ROS scavenger during the photo-Fenton-like catalytic process. The scavengers used were methanol (MeOH, 1.5 mM) for the hydroxyl radical (
OH), ascorbic acid (AA, 1.5 mM) for the superoxide radical (O2
−), sodium azide (NaN3, 1.5 mM) for the singlet oxygen (1O2), respectively.

The drug resistance of intermediates during the TC degrading process was tested by four kinds of bacteria strains: Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC25923), E. coli (ATCC25922), Staphylococcus epidermidis (CMCC26069) and Bacillus subtilis. The cell culture methods were used as described by Liu [42].

The cytotoxicity of catalysts was assessed via the growth of the mouse Sertoli cell lines (TM4) and the human epithelial cell lines (A549) (purchased from Tianjin Ding Guo Biotechnology co. LTD, China). Cell viability was evaluated by cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay (Dojindo, Japan) [43]. Each determination was conducted in triplicate. More details are available in supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of CQDs/α-FeOOH nanohybrids and α-FeOOH
ce:section.ce:para: Goethite, as a type of orthorhombic iron oxyhydroxide mineral in relation to its crystallographic structure, played an important role in environmental adsorption or catalysis. The sharp X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. 1 a) indicated well developed crystalline nature of fresh synthesized α-FeOOH, CQDs/α-FeOOH and CQDs samples. Characteristic diffraction peaks of CQDs/α-FeOOH nanocomposites or α-FeOOH nanocrystal located at 2θ = 21.2°, 33.2°, 36.6°, 41.3° and 53.3° corresponding to the (110), (130), (111), (140) and (221) planes of the orthogonal phase of goethite (JCPDS#29-0713) [14], respectively. There was no shifting of diffraction peaks and no other impurity peaks are observed indicating the purity of the as-prepared CQDs/α-FeOOH. The typical diffraction broad peak of CQDs is at around 2θ = 26.2° which might be ascribed to (002) lattice plane of hexagonal carbon skeleton (JCPDS No. 75-1621). However, the absence of the diffraction peaks within CQDs/α-FeOOH nanohybrid corresponding to CQDs was probably ascribed to low doping amount, suggesting that the introduction of CQDs almost made no differences on α-FeOOH diffraction [33,44,45].

FTIR spectra were conducted to further investigate the interaction between CQDs and α-FeOOH. The pristine α-FeOOH FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1b) displayed two characteristic peaks centered at 3384 cm-1 and 3153 cm-1 assigned to the stretching mode of hydrogen-bonded water molecules and hydrophilic hydroxyls function groups, as well as a typical peak at 1637 cm-1 is associated with the deformation vibration of H2O or OH, indicating the presence of OH and adsorbed water in α-FeOOH [28,46,47]. In addition, the absorption bands at 1141 cm-1, 1047 cm-1 and 977 cm-1 could be indexed to the active vibrations and split stretching mode of SO4, corresponding with several peaks in the range of 1200−900 cm-1 implying that the existence of sulfate complex in iron oxide phase [12]. Additionally, two apparent peaks measured at 890 cm-1 and 795 cm-1 were ascribed to bending vibration of Fe-O-H bond and a shoulder at 631 cm-1 was due to Fe-O stretching vibration relying on this structure of α-FeOOH [48,49]. Comparing with FTIR analysis of α-FeOOH, there appeared new features for CQDs/α-FeOOH, a small shoulder at 2922 cm-1 was connected with CH stretching and one absorption band recorded at 1200 cm-1 owing to COC bringing stretching [6]. Meanwhile, new minor peaks within the scope of 1700−1100 cm-1 may associated with CO vibration [12]. The emergence of carbonyl groups in CQDs/α-FeOOH therefore obviously implied the interfacial interaction between two materials.

Raman spectrums depicted in Fig. 1c have also been employed to collect the additional structure information on the interaction between CQDs and α-FeOOH. For pristine α-FeOOH, the typical peaks in the range of 0−1000 nm were attributed to the iron oxide structure [50]. CQDs showed the existence of two Raman peaks at 1343 cm-1 and 1585 cm-1 could be indexed to d-band (disordered sp2 carbon) and G-band (conjugated sp2 carbon atoms) [51], respectively. It was worth mentioning that the relatively weak vibrational modes of α-FeOOH could be observed in CQDs/α-FeOOH composites, along with two other Raman bands which were well matched with CQDs, revealing the successful introduction of CQDs into α-FeOOH matrix.

The morphology of CQDs and CQDs/α-FeOOH samples was observed through TEM images. TEM image of the size distribution of CQDs in Fig. S1a showed that the CQDs appeared a spherical-like structure with diameters ranging from 1 to 4 nm and mean diameter of 1.84 ± 0.3 nm (Fig. S1b). Hollow sea urchin-like shaped CQDs/α-FeOOH (Fig. 1d-1e) comprised of uniform spicule structure with around 450 nm in length and 40 nm in width, owing to the α-FeOOH crystalline. The HRTEM image and the existence of α-FeOOH with fast Fourier transformation (FFT, inset of Fig. 1f) presented the d-spacing values were calculated as 0.245 nm and 0.340 nm, corresponding to the (111) lattice plane of α-FeOOH and (002) plane of graphite carbon, proving up-conversion CQDs/α-FeOOH nanohybrids were successful formation through α-FeOOH nanocrystals self-assembling with incorporation of CQDs. It is desired that the presence of up-converted CQDs within the matrix of α-FeOOH would enable α-FeOOH to exhibit highly efficient visible-light-responsive properties and remarkable oxidation efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs enhanced photodegradation ability of α-FeOOH in Fenton-like system
ce:section.ce:para:
The photo-Fenton catalytic performance of as-synthesized samples was evaluated via removal or degradation of TC under various experimental conditions. In Fig. 2 a, the TC removal ratio in CQDs/α-FeOOH adsorption test after 60 min was 3.1 % indicating the poor TC concentrating or adsorption ability by the as-prepared catalyst. TC degradation efficiency in H2O2+Vis oxidation test was about 6.1 %, reflecting that H2O2 could hardly be triggered by visible light alone to produce O
H and the visible light triggered photolysis of TC could practically be ignored [52]. Contrast to that in H2O2+Vis system, the TC degradation efficiency in CQDs/α-FeOOH catalyzed H2O2 system shows great improvement (ca. 74.3 %). Compared with TC degradation ratio in α-FeOOH+H2O2+Vis heterogeneous Fenton-like system (ca. 80.1 %), TC degradation ratio in CQDs/α-FeOOH+H2O2+Vis system exhibited a dramatically enhancement and achieved 94.5 % within 60 min revealing the important role of CQDs in regulating or even enhancing the visible light responsive catalytic performance of pristine α-FeOOH due to CQDs’ fast electron transformation ability [26,53] and enhanced TC concentrating ability around α-FeOOH nanocrystals.

It is generally accepted that the heterogeneous catalytic degradation reaction could be described by the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model.

Where r0, k1, k2 and K were total reaction rate, apparent reaction rate constant, adsorption constant and pseudo-first order apparent rate constant (min-1), respectively, c and t denoted concentration of pollutants (mg L-1) and time (min). The corresponding kinetics rate constants of TC degradation in various conditions in Fig. 2a were fitted with simplified Langmuir-Hinshelwood model in Eq. (3) and results were shown In Fig. 2b. TC has a highest rate constant of 0.1525 min-1 (R2 = 0.960) in CQDs/α-FeOOH+H2O2+Vis system, approximately 3.7 times than that of α-FeOOH+H2O2+Vis (0.0415 min-1, R2 = 0.994), and 2.4 times that of CQDs/α-FeOOH+H2O2, 12.9 times that of CQDs/α-FeOOH + Vis, 54.9 times that of H2O2+Vis. With regard to the TC antibiotics removal ratio within the same time period under visible light, our CQDs/α-FeOOH also shows superior higher photocatalytic efficiency contrast to previous reported photocatalyst, such as Ag/N-GQDs/g-C3N4 (92.8 %) [54], CQDs/Bi2MoO6 (51 %) [33], and exhibited almost similar performance compared to core-shell Ag2CrO4/N-GQDs@g-C3N4 (95.3 %) [5]. The greatly enhanced TC removal ratio in the synergistic CQDs/α-FeOOH+H2O2+Vis heterogeneous photo-Fenton system can be mainly due to the unique up-converted PL effect of CQDs.

It is worth mentioning that the activation ratios of H2O2 in α-FeOOH+H2O2+Vis and CQDs/α-FeOOH +H2O2+Vis systems (displayed in Fig. 2c) reach to 83.4 % and 93.0 %, separately, confirming the incorporation of CQDs substantially enhanced the activation ability of H2O2 by α-FeOOH. The mineralization of TC reached 59.6 % after 60 min in CQDs/α-FeOOH+H2O2 systems under visible light irradiation in Fig. 2d. The decomposition trend of H2O2 was consistent with that of TC removal trend in α-FeOOH+H2O2+Vis system. While the decomposition trend of H2O2 was completely different with that of TC removal trend in CQDs/α-FeOOH+H2O2+Vis system, for example, in CQDs/α-FeOOH + Vis+H2O2 system, the decomposition rate of H2O2 within 5 min reached to ca. 6.4 % but the degradation rate attained ca. 76.1 %, which may be attributed to conjugated CQDs and the interaction between CQDs and α-FeOOH thereby allowing H2O2 to be available to CQDs/α-FeOOH catalyst.

The α-FeOOH with different CQDs doping amounts (0.7 wt %, 1 wt %, 3 wt %, 5 wt %) were fabricated to explore its effect on the degradation of TC in CQDs/α-FeOOH+H2O2+Vis system depicted in Fig. 3 a. The degradation efficiency of TC increased from 80 %, 80.2 % to 94.5 % after 60 min with the more CQDs doping within α-FeOOH increasing from 0 wt %, 0.7 wt % to 1 wt %, respectively. However, continuing increase CQDs doping amount from 1 wt % to 3 wt % or 5 wt % will lead to the gradually decrease of the TC degradation efficiencies. It reasonably indicates that the proper amount of CQDs is in favor of the enhancement of the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/FeOOH. This phenomenon is similar to wang’s group [30], Di’ group [31], Hu’ group [55] etc. The enhanced TC degradation efficiencies could be assigned to more electron-hole pairs and O
H generated along with the increase of CQDs doping amounts in this system. Therefore, 1 wt % was chosen as the proper CQDs doping amount in the following experiment, which was comparable with previously reported photocatalysts including CQDs/Bi2MoO6 (2 wt %) [33] and CQDs/BiOX (3 wt %) [32].

The oxidant concentration in photo-Fenton reaction is essential for contaminant degradation. Fig. 3b exhibited the TC degradation efficiency evidently improved along with the H2O2 molar concentration increased from 0.125 mM to 0.50 mM, due to the massive generation of O
H under the cooperation of CQDs/α-FeOOH and visible light. While H2O2 further increased to 0.75 mM, it appeared a slightly enhancement on TC degradation, this may be caused by scavenging effect of H2O2 (H2O2+
OH→H2O+HO2
) [12]. Excessive H2O2 also accompanied with increased operating cost for practical use, thus 0.50 mM H2O2, equal to the molar ratios of H2O2 to TC of 12, appeared to be more efficient in this work. The H2O2 consumption in our work is much fewer than massive other similar photo-Fenton-like reactions, for instance, the molar ratio of H2O2 to pollutants was 235 for TC removal catalyzed by NiFe2O4/C [56], was 257 for 4-chlorophenol removal catalyzed by FeO(OH)/reduced graphene oxide aerogel [10], was 5068 for methyl orange removal catalyzed by Fe2O3/graphene aerogels [57], and was 30 for phenol removal catalyzed by α-FeOOH/mesoporous carbon [3], respectively. These further indicate the economic applicability of this system.

Fig. 3c evaluated initial pH value as an essential factor influence TC degradation in CQDs/α-FeOOH+H2O2+Vis system. The initial pH values directly affected the surface properties of CQDs/α-FeOOH and existing form of TC which was in the form of protonated (TCH3 +, pH＜3.3), neutral (TCH2 0, pH 3.3–7.7), monoanionic (TCH3-, pH＞7.7), separately [2]. The zeta potential value demonstrated that the zero-charge point of CQDs/α-FeOOH was 8.8 shown in inset of Fig. 3c. When the pH values below 7.7 all degradation efficiencies of TC achieved to above 90 % after 60 min due to more O
H produced by the activation of H2O2 [3,10]; 2) π-π interaction between TCH3 + or TCH2 0 form with the conjugated π system of CQDs will benefit to concentrate TC around the surface of CQDs/α-FeOOH. When pH value increased from 7.7–11, a significant decrease appeared in this system resulting from less generated free radicals, the competition between OH- and TCH3- for activated sites on CQDs/α-FeOOH, and electrostatic repulsion between monoanionic form of TCH3- and negatively charged CQDs/α-FeOOH surface [2]. Meanwhile, Fig. S2 described the decomposition of H2O2 during TC degradation at different pH values in CQDs/α-FeOOH+H2O2+Vis system. The influence of pH on H2O2 decomposition ratio was consistent with that of corresponded TC degradation efficiency in this system implying that H2O2 was mainly applied to TC degradation rather than self-decomposition. The decomposition of H2O2 reduced from 93.0 % to 44.0 % when the pH value changed from 3.09–10.31, revealing that H2O2 was more prone to generate considerable amount of O
H under acidic conditions [11]. CQDs/α-FeOOH as the heterogeneous photo-Fenton catalyst maintained an outstanding catalytic performance within an extensively wide pH range. And the excellent catalytic performance at approximate neutral pH make it good candidate for practical waste treatment [10], revealing the CQDs/α-FeOOH+H2O2+Vis could effectively overcome the shortcomings of limited pH range existing in the traditional homogeneous Fenton system.

Fig. S3 presented the photocatalytic degradation performance of α-FeOOH and as-prepared CQDs/α-FeOOH materials via degrading three types of organic contaminants with different surface charges, containing positively charged methylene blue (MB), negatively charged Orange II (OII), and neutral molecular TC under IR, visible, UV light irradiation in order to test the potential of CQDs/α-FeOOH utilized as a versatile photocatalyst in treating different type of contaminants under solar spectrum. Fig. 3d showed the pseudo-first order kinetic degradation rate of MB, OII and TC in CQDs/α-FeOOH+H2O2+Vis system under various irradiation sources. The degradation of MB, OII are well conformed with Langmuir-Hinshelwood mechanism, implying the generated active radicals on the surface of catalysts were involved with the depletion of absorbed TC, MB, OII [12,58]. As far as irradiation sources, CQDs/α-FeOOH exhibited an apparently enhanced degradation rates of TC, MB, and OII in photo-Fenton-like systems than that of α-FeOOH under UV–vis-IR spectrum irradiation, indicating the positive role of CQDs in regulating the photocatalytic properties of α-FeOOH under full spectrum, especially greatly improved degradation rates of contaminants under Vis-IR irradiation. As for three types of organic contaminants, the relatively higher TC degradation kinetic could be ascribed to the effect of π-π interaction between TC and CQDs [26,59]. It can be seen that the degradation of contaminants depended more on structure of organic pollutants and irradiation sources. Herein, it could be concluded that the incorporation of CQDs with α-FeOOH could boost the photo-Fenton catalytic efficiency of various surface charged organic contaminants due to unique tunable PL property of CQDs to expand visible-infrared-responsive range.

In Fig. 3e, the durability of CQDs/α-FeOOH was evaluated after recycling 5 times for TC degradation in CQDs/α-FeOOH+H2O2+Vis system. TC degradation in this system slightly decreased after 5 cycle reuses. The FTIR spectra of CQDs/α-FeOOH (Fig. S4) before and after photo-Fenton reaction showed no evident changes in the characteristic peaks of as-prepared catalyst. The above results signified the excellent photo-catalytical performance and structural stability of CQDs/α-FeOOH, which was might ascribed to the strongly chemical interaction between α-FeOOH and CQDs. Moreover, the amount of leaching Fe in Fig. 3e decreased from 0.63 to 0.02 mg L-1 from first cycle to 5th cycle reuse, which was evidently lower than European wastewater discharge standard of 2 ppm [2]. This also demonstrated CQDs/α-FeOOH could act as a mechanical stable photocatalyst to activate H2O2 in eliminating organic contaminants. Owing to the significant role of iron contents played in the homogeneous Fenton process, and some researches elucidated that there existed chelation behavior between tetracycline and Fe2+ or Fe3+ as aquo-complexes [60]. So as to further evaluate the contribution of homogeneous Fenton-like in TC degradation derived from the leaked iron in this system, the maximum leaked Fe concentration of 0.63 mg L-1 was chosen as the source of homogeneous iron to activate H2O2 for photodegrading TC. The result (Fig. 3f) showed merely 18.5 % TC was removed in homogeneous Fenton reaction, apparently lower than that in heterogeneous CQDs/α-FeOOH + Vis+H2O2 system, and slightly higher than that in H2O2+Vis system, revealing the mainly contribution of TC degradation from CQDs/α-FeOOH + Vis+H2O2 system rather than that homogeneous Fe leaking + Vis+H2O2 system.

A series of quenching tests described in Fig. 4 a were conducted to identify the reactive oxygen species (ROS) i.e. ·OH, O2 ·-, 1O2 produced in the CQDs/α-FeOOH+H2O2+Vis system employing corresponding MeOH, AA and NaN3 radical scavengers [10,12], respectively. It could be observed that the degradation of TC was obviously declined in the existence of MeOH, AA and NaN3 compared to that without scavengers, proving that ·OH, O2 ·- and 1O2 were involved in this heterogeneous photo-Fenton-like reaction. The inhibitory effect of MeOH addition indicated the TC degradation was driven by ·OH radicals via the H2O2 activation by CQDs/α-FeOOH under visible-light irritation [10]. O2 ·- acted as an important intermediary for the ·OH radicals generation and transformation during superoxide-driven reaction [61], which was formed through the reduction of adsorbed oxygen by photoinduced electrons. Simultaneously, the oxidation of Fe2+ on the surface of catalyst accompanied with conversion of O2
- to OH
O 2
. -
+ 2
H +
+ e →
H 2
O 2
,
H 2
O 2
+ F
e
2 +
→ F
e
3 +
+ .
O H + O
H -
) [12,62]. The quenching effect of NaN3 suggested that 1O2 also played an important role in this photo-Fenton system. It has been reported through the conversion of O2 ·-, 1O2 could form and function as a reactive part on TC degradation [34,63]. The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra shown in Fig. 4b-4d were also applied to further reverify the results of quenching tests. Both the ESR signals intensity were too weak to be observed under dark condition. However, the 1:2:2:1 four typical signals of DMPO-·OH, and signal of DMPO-O2- and TEMP-1O2 spin-trap reagents appeared and gradually strengthened as the prolonged irradiation time, revealing the generation of ·OH, O2 ·- and 1O2 radicals in CQDs/α-FeOOH+H2O2+Vis system with time. The consequences confirmed that O2-, OH, 1O2 were the major ROS responsible for TC degradation in this photo-Fenton-like reaction.

Up-converted photoluminescence (PL) measurement is generally used to analyze the converted range and intensity between excitation and emission spectra for tunable fluorescent material. In Fig. 5 a, Up-converted PL spectra of CQDs show shorter emission wavelengths (450−650 nm) while triggered by longer wavelengths (600−800 nm), exhibiting brilliant up-conversion fluorescent merits of CQDs. Based on the superior optical properties CQDs possess, it can be speculated that the incorporation of CQDs will greatly boost photo-catalytical performance and broaden solar spectrum utilization of α-FeOOH. The UV–vis absorption spectra are extensively applied to describe the optical absorption ability and band gaps of semiconductors. UV–vis DRS were measured comparatively to discriminate the efficiency of photocatalytic activity of CQDs/α-FeOOH and α-FeOOH depicted in Fig. 5b. The light-absorption edge of α-FeOOH is 604 nm, and the absorption edge of CQDs/α-FeOOH shows an evident red-shift extending responsive spectrum from 604 nm to 637 nm. Furthermore, CQDs/α-FeOOH exhibits a stronger light-absorption intensity in contrast to α-FeOOH especially in visible area owing to the excellent light up-converted responsive merits of CQDs [34]. According to Equation of
E =
h × c
λ
where E is band gap energy, h, c and λ separately present Plank’s constant (6.626 × 10-34 J·s), velocity of light (3 × 108 m s-1) and absorption wavelength (nm), the band gap energy of CQDs/α-FeOOH was calculated to be 1.94 eV which is narrower than that of α-FeOOH (2.06 eV). The introduced CQDs may contribute to the capture of longer wavelength further facilitate the formation of electron-hole pairs [34,64]. The valence band (VB) of CQDs/α-FeOOH was determined to be 2.10 eV vs NHE shown in Fig. S5, the conduction band position (CB) was about 0.16 eV vs NHE, which can be calculated by ECB=EVB-Eg. It means CQDs/α-FeOOH can be triggered by 637−800 nm of visible light which can be converted to below 637 nm due to the up-converted PL of CQDs to produce electrons and holes. The produced electrons can reduce Fe (III) to Fe (II) (the potential of Fe (III)/Fe (II) is 0.771 V) increasing the cycling of iron species in Fenton reaction. Fig. 5c presents steady-state PL spectra of α-FeOOH and CQDs/α-FeOOH. The relative stronger intensity of the observed PL reflect the recombination probability of photo-excited electron-hole pairs [65]. Herein, it is clearly seen that two materials displays strong emission shoulder at around 410 nm, and CQDs/α-FeOOH exhibits an apparently weaker PL intensity due to the great electronic storage capacity of CQDs. CQDs served as the source and sink of electrons thereby suppressing the recombination of electrons and holes. The slower electron-hole pairs recombination is beneficial to Fe (III)/Fe (II) cycle leading to promote catalytic efficiency for Fenton reaction [3].

Unlike the massive absorption capacity of graphene-based and mesoporous material [2,8], it confirmed that the introduction of CQDs would not significantly promote the adsorption capacity for TC (shown in Fig. 1). Thus, it is the superior optical properties and efficient electronic transportation of CQDs that contribute most to the promotion of photocatalytic performance of TC in this heterogeneous photo-Fenton system.

Besides, incorporation of CQDs act as a significant role in CQDs/α-FeOOH because of efficiently accelerating the photo-generated carrier separation and transfer leading to the enhanced degradation performance [20,64]. In order to further confirm this result, the transient photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests are conducted, which is always employed as favorable proof to evaluate the photoinduced charge migration of the samples. In transient photocurrent response spectra showed in Fig. 5d, CQDs/α-FeOOH achieves a higher photocurrent response than that of pristine α-FeOOH under the Visible-light radiation. And it retains a steady trend during the four intermittent radiation cycles, the phenomenon means CQDs/α-FeOOH material possesses a higher separation velocity of Visible-light-excited carriers which can be assigned to the unique up-converted PL of CQDs. Moreover, the photoinduced electrons excited from valence band firstly transfer to the conduction band of CQDs/α-FeOOH and then migrate to the CQDs, thereby directly impairing the recombination of electron-hole pairs [30,55]. In electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) shown in Fig. 5e, the radius of a half arc that affects the internal surface charge migration rate occurring at the catalyst. The smaller size arc radius of the semi-arc in the EIS Nyquist plot indicates that the charge transfer resistance at the electrode interface of CQDs/α-FeOOH is relatively lower than that of α-FeOOH, suggesting a faster internal surface charge migration rate due to lower impedance value [30], which is exactly consistent with PL results. Aforementioned analytical methods indirectly prove the electronic interaction between CQDs and α-FeOOH. Besides, Xinhe Bao’s group used density function theory (DFT) calculations to reveal this electronic interaction between activate component and support/dopant for the as-prepared carbon nanotubes encapsulated iron nanoparticles from the aspect of theory; and simultaneously directly found the local electronic structures of carbon shells significantly modified or enhanced by iron nanoparticles due to the hybridization of unoccupied 3d orbitals from iron particles with carbon 2p orbitals through a combination of the scanning transmission X-ray microscopy followed with X-ray absorption spectral calculations from the aspect of experimental evidence [66]. Aforementioned results can help us understand the nature of this quite promising visible-light-responsive heterogeneous catalytic performance of CQDs/α-FeOOH in decontamination of aqueous TC be due to the strong electronic interaction between the active α-FeOOH and CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug resistance tests of TC degradation intermediates and cytotoxicity assay of α-FeOOH and CQDs/α-FeOOH
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorbance changes during TC degradation in CQDs/α-FeOOH++H2O2+Vis system were shown in Fig. S6. There was a drastically declination of main UV–vis absorption peaks at 270 nm and 357 nm of TC after 5 min, and after 60 min there was no significant absorption could be observed indicating the complete elimination of TC in this system. TC became more vulnerable to ROS attack resulting in hydroxylation product and further aromatic ring breaking [67]. TC degradation intermediates in CQDs/α-FeOOH+H2O2+Vis system after 60 min were verified by liquid chromatograph coupled with a mass spectrometer shown in Fig. S7 [68]. The degradation of TC in this system begins with the oxidation amino-containing groups by hydroxylation of TC with m/z = 444 as the dominant reaction pathway, resulting in the formation of fragments corresponding to m/z = 474, 421, 390, 370 and 322, and finally TC can be mineralized into small molecules, CO2 and H2O. The proposed TC degradation pathway was shown in Fig. S7. In general, the oxidation of contaminants accompanied with toxicity change for living organisms while sometimes the degradation intermediates will be more toxic than the mother compounds. The toxicity of TC degradation intermediates in CQDs/α-FeOOH+H2O2+Vis system was assessed via luminescent bacteria (Vibrio qinghaiensis) shown in Fig. S8 [69]. The relative luminosity decreased from 13.9 % to 1.3 % after 60 min, implying that the toxicity of the intermediates increased with TC degradation process before 40 min, and then the toxicity of the intermediates decreased with reaction time until 60 min indicting the continuous elimination of the toxic intermediates of TC. It was worth mentioning that the anti-drug resistance of TC related degradation intermediates was completely eliminated within this process.

The bacterial inhibition experiments of four bacteria strains ATCC25923, ATCC25922, CMCC26069 and Bacillus subtilis susceptibility to TC were used to evaluate the drug resistance of intermediates of TC in this system. The relative toxicity of degradation intermediates of TC with time could be assessed through the diameter of the inhibition zones shown in Fig. 6 a-6d. Gram-positive bacteria Bacillus subtilis, ATCC25923 and CMCC26069 presented 9.1 mm, 8.5 mm, 9.5 mm inhibition zone at an initial TC concentration, while no inhibition zone was observed for Gram-negative bacteria ATCC25922, owing that Gram-positive bacteria performed more sensitive or susceptible to TC than that of Gram-negative bacteria. Fast disappearance of inhibition zone of TC degradation intermediates implied no observed drug tolerance effects on these bacteria by TC degradation intermediates. The results confirmed the excellent efficiency of CQDs/α-FeOOH catalyzed Fenton-like reaction in eliminating TC and its related drug resistance without producing second pollutants.

Considering the practical applications of nanoparticles, the CCK-8 assay was performed to assess the biocompatibility of α-FeOOH and CQDs/α-FeOOH shown in Fig. 6e-6f. The TM4 cell viability almost made no obvious differences with the increasing CQDs/α-FeOOH concentrations; while TM4 cell viability has about 30 % decrease with increasing α-FeOOH concentrations. The A549 cell viability decreased 16 % and 29 % with the increasing concentration of CQDs/α-FeOOH or α-FeOOH, respectively. However, CQDs/α-FeOOH maintained a relatively higher A549 cell viability than in presence of α-FeOOH. The results of the relative cell viability of two types of cells suggested low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility of CQDs/α-FeOOH due to the incorporation of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TC degradation in different water matrices
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further test the practical application of CQDs/α-FeOOH, the degradation of TC in different water matrices was carried out under Visible-light or real sun-light irradiation at outdoor climate condition, which was presented in Fig. 7 . Contrast to TC degradation in CQDs/α-FeOOH+H2O2+Vis (350 W Xe with 420 nm filter) system in various water matrices, the TC degradation under real climate sunlight in DI water slightly decreased 7.7 %, in Yangtze river or WWTP water slightly increased 3.2 % and 7.4 %, and in East China Sea water decreased 14.4 %, respectively. The TC degradation in this system in various water matrices under visible light basically can be compared with that of in real climate condition without obvious deterioration. The declined performance could be assigned to the dissolved organic matter (DOM) in water matrices, which would consume part of ROS due to the competition between DOM and target contaminants for active sites on the surface of catalyst [70,71]. Besides, the relatively low TC degradation in East China Sea water might due to high salinity shown in Table. S1 [34,70]. Overall, CQDs/α-FeOOH+H2O2 system actually exhibited fairly good performance on TC removal in real climate sun-light condition. It can be seen that the incorporation of CQDs remarkably enhances the photocatalytic property of α-FeOOH and broaden the utilization of solar light spectrum. The possible photo-Fenton reaction mechanism of CQDs/α-FeOOH catalyst is proposed in Fig. 8 .

CQDs/α-FeOOH nanohybrids displayed enhanced photocatalytic performance on TC degradation as compared to pristine α-FeOOH under visible-light irradiation (λ＞420 nm) in presence of relatively lower amount of H2O2, which was ascribed to the up-converted effect of CQDs. The CQDs/α-FeOOH+H2O2 system also exhibited broad application pH range, effective removal efficiencies on various organic contaminants with different structure and surface charges under UV–vis-IR irradiations, excellent durability and can complete eliminate the TC and its related drug tolerance. From the aspects of environmental application, CQDs/α-FeOOH also performed good catalytic performance on TC degradation in different water matrices under real solar-light irradiation. Considering the low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility of CQDs/α-FeOOH possessed, CQDs/α-FeOOH can be used as an environmental-friendly and highly efficiently photo-Fenton catalyst to deal with other antibiotics utilizing the real solar light radiation.
